summary: Jeff Atwood shares his frustration with forgetting XPath syntax between infrequent uses and his search for Aaron Skonnard's interactive XPath Expression Builder tool. After discovering the original tool had been moved and converted to inconvenient zip file downloads, he decided to host the interactive web pages on his own server. The post serves as both a personal note and a public service for developers who similarly struggle with XPath syntax. He provides links to both the classic 1.0 and improved 4.0 versions of the tool.
key_points: Infrequently used syntax (like XPath) is easily forgotten between uses
Interactive tools with real-time visual feedback are valuable for learning and remembering syntax
Distributing tools as zip files ruins the immediate accessibility that made them useful
Sometimes the best solution is to host useful resources yourself when original sources become inconvenient
money_quotes: I use Xpath queries about once a year, so of course I completely forget the syntax every time I come back to it.
And they're now distributed as zip files, which completely ruins the immediate feedback that made the interactive XPath builder pages so useful.
key_insight: Interactive tools with immediate feedback are invaluable for working with syntax you use too infrequently to memorize.
